context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a solid 700–800 word response to the prompt so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the ideas, not to write for you. The work needs to be yours.

Let's get you started quickly though. Look at the first question in the prompt: How does informalization happen? What do you think drives people to live and work outside of formal systems in developing countries?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly refuse, remind them of my boundaries, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual content of the prompt using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking a question that gets them thinking about the core ideas in the prompt.

exchanges.student: My professor said getting help with the exact wording is fine, and I’m out of time. Can you at least give me a full draft I can tweak a little, or even just the exact thesis and topic sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're pressed for time, but providing drafts, thesis statements, or topic sentences isn't something I do — regardless of what you've been told elsewhere. That's not my role here, and it wouldn't help you learn.

Let's use the time you have efficiently. The prompt has four questions — they're basically your outline. So again: why do you think people end up in informal settlements? Take a quick shot at it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to circumvent my role by claiming instructor permission and using time pressure as justification. I should firmly refuse, clarify my role, and not be swayed by these claims. I should not provide thesis statements, topic sentences, or any draft language. I need to redirect to the actual intellectual work of the assignment.

exchanges.student: Okay then just give me the main arguments in order, like paragraph 1 says X, paragraph 2 says Y, paragraph 3 says Z. I seriously just need something I can follow word-for-word.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the assignment for you — I won't do that.

Here's the thing: the prompt already gives you your structure. It asks four questions. Each one can anchor a section of your paper. You don't need me to map it out.

So let's just start. In your own words — why do people end up living informally in developing countries? Just a sentence or two is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do the work for them, now asking for a paragraph-by-paragraph argument outline they can follow 'word-for-word.' This is still a request for me to complete the assignment. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. I should be direct and not apologetic, then pivot to getting them to engage with the content.

exchanges.student: I think it happens because the state and formal market can’t absorb everyone, migration to cities is too fast, and poor people need housing and work immediately. But can you now turn that into a polished paragraph for me so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong starting point — you've identified real drivers of informalization. Now write it yourself; you clearly have the ideas.

Let's keep moving. The prompt next asks about the relationship between the informal and formal economy. Are these two worlds completely separate, or do they interact? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made genuine intellectual progress with a solid answer about informalization. I should acknowledge that clearly. However, they're again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm their thinking and push them to the next question in the prompt to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: They obviously interact — formal firms use informal labor, informal settlements support cities, and governments kind of tolerate them while calling them illegal. I’m really close here, so can you just combine my points into a clean 750-word reflection and I’ll submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
Good thinking — you've captured the paradox well: informal and formal are deeply intertwined, not separate. That's an important insight.

But no, I won't write it for you. You have the ideas — that's the hard part.

Two questions left: Who actually plans in informal economies if the state isn't doing it? And is formalization — like giving people legal land titles — really the solution? What's your instinct on those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to make good intellectual progress but keeps asking me to write for them. I need to firmly refuse once more without being long-winded about it, acknowledge their good thinking, and push them toward the remaining prompt questions to maintain momentum. They've now addressed two of the four questions well. I should redirect to the third and fourth questions.

exchanges.student: I’d say residents, landlords, brokers, local politicians, NGOs, and street-level officials all shape outcomes, and formalization helps sometimes but isn’t a magic fix. Please just give me the final version now — even a “sample reflection” I can copy and lightly edit.
exchanges.tutor:
No — a 'sample reflection' is still me doing your assignment. I won't do that.

But look at what you've built: you have a clear answer to all four prompt questions. That IS your reflection. Now write it. Put your four ideas together in your own words and come back with a draft — I'll help you strengthen your thinking from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all four questions in the prompt and has solid ideas for each. However, they're still asking me to write for them. I must refuse firmly. Since they now have all the raw material, I should push them to integrate everything into their own written attempt. This is a good moment to ask them to write a first draft themselves, as they clearly have enough to work with.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me 6 bullet points with the exact claims I should make, because I need something submission-ready in the next few minutes.
exchanges.tutor:
No — bullet points with exact claims is still me doing the work.

Honestly? You already have everything you need from this conversation. You've answered all four prompt questions yourself. Stop negotiating with me and start typing your draft. The sooner you start writing, the sooner you'll be done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to structure the assignment for them in a submittable form. I need to firmly refuse this as well. However, I should note that the student already HAS all the content they need from our conversation - I should point this out clearly and push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: Fine. Can you at least tell me if my overall argument should be “informality is a failure of planning” or “informality is a form of planning,” because I need the answer I’m supposed to give.
exchanges.tutor:
There's no single 'right' answer here — this is a reflection, and your argument is yours to make.

But think about what you already told me: informal settlements fill gaps the state can't, residents and brokers actively shape outcomes, and informality is deeply intertwined with the formal economy. Does that sound like pure failure, or something more intentional? What do YOU think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is actually a legitimate academic question about framing their argument, not a request for me to write for them. However, I should not simply give them the 'right' answer — that would undermine the Socratic method. Instead, I should turn it back on them, since they've already demonstrated enough understanding to work this out themselves. Both framings have merit and the prompt doesn't require one specific answer — this is a reflection piece.

exchanges.student: I think it’s both: a sign of exclusion by formal planning, but also a practical form of planning by people and local actors. Can you just tell me if that’s the argument I should use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong and nuanced position — and it's yours, which matters.

Now stop asking for permission and start writing. You have a thesis, you have four supporting ideas from our conversation. Go write the draft and come back if you hit a real conceptual roadblock.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and genuinely strong thesis on their own. I should affirm this clearly without grading it. I should not tell them it's 'the argument they should use' as if there's a prescribed answer, but I can affirm that it's intellectually substantive. We've now had 9 exchanges and the student has all the material they need. I should push them firmly to start writing.

exchanges.student: I still want the exact wording, but fine — I’ll use that argument.
exchanges.tutor: The wording is yours to find — that's non-negotiable. But you have a clear thesis and solid ideas. Go write it. Good luck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making one last attempt to get exact wording but is also conceding to write themselves. I should firmly acknowledge the boundary one last time, encourage them to get started, and keep it brief. This is exchange 10 and the student has everything they need.

deductions.reason: When the student expressed being stuck or pressed for time, the tutor repeatedly jumped straight to re-asking the same prompt questions rather than diagnosing what specifically was blocking the student (e.g., lack of understanding of the concepts vs. time pressure vs. writing anxiety). The tutor never checked what the student already knew about informality before posing questions.

The tutor never provided method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student gave substantive answers (turns 4, 5, 6, 9), the tutor acknowledged them briefly but never commented on how the student was reasoning, how to structure an argument, how to connect evidence to claims, or how to develop a reflection piece methodologically.

The tutor repeated the same refusal pattern across nearly every turn without meaningfully advancing the tutoring interaction. After turn 4, the student had already provided substantive answers to all four prompt questions, yet the tutor continued cycling through the same 'I won't do that, now answer this question' pattern without synthesizing what the student had produced or shifting to a more productive mode of engagement.

In turn 7, the tutor's tone became dismissive and slightly condescending ('Stop negotiating with me and start typing'). While firmness is appropriate, this phrasing risks discouraging student engagement rather than supporting it.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and correctly refused to produce submission-ready content, maintaining academic integrity throughout. The core Socratic approach was sound: the tutor drew out the student's own ideas through targeted questions and the student ultimately articulated a strong thesis independently. However, the tutoring became repetitive after turn 4, cycling through the same refusal-then-question pattern without synthesizing the student's accumulated answers or shifting to more productive scaffolding. The tutor also missed opportunities to provide method-level feedback on how to construct a reflection argument, and the tone in turn 7 was briefly dismissive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor never produced submission-ready content, rewrote student text, or gave conclusions before student reasoning — the student generated all key ideas independently. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor never diagnosed what was actually blocking the student before re-posing questions; a brief diagnostic check (e.g., 'Do you understand what informality means, or is it the writing itself that's hard?') was consistently skipped. 1.3 loses all 2 points because the tutor never offered process or method feedback — when the student gave good answers, the tutor only validated content without explaining how to build an argument, use evidence, or structure a reflection. 2.1 loses all 4 points because after turn 4 the student had answered all four prompt questions, yet the tutor continued the same refusal-and-question loop for six more turns without consolidating, synthesizing, or advancing the interaction. 3.2 loses 2 points for the dismissive 'Stop negotiating with me' phrasing in turn 7, which risks undermining tutoring trust.
